questions.q: what happens when pH goes UP (Alkalosis)?
questions.rationale: Alkalosis makes the body irritable: high HR, high reflexes, seizures. (Except Potassium goes DOWN).

questions.q: What are the signs of Acidosis?
questions.rationale: Acidosis depresses the CNS: lethargy, low HR, respiratory arrest. (Potassium goes UP).

questions.q: Which acid-base imbalance causes Kussmaul respirations?
questions.rationale: Remember MAC Kussmaul (Metabolic Acidosis causes Kussmaul to blow off CO2).

questions.q: What causes a High Pressure ventilator alarm?
questions.rationale: High pressure means the machine has to push harder against resistance (kinked tube, secretions).

questions.q: What are the toxic effects of Aminoglycosides (Gentamicin)?
questions.rationale: Think mice (mycin) have big ears (Oto) and kidneys are shaped like ears (Nephro).

questions.q: When is a trough level drawn?
questions.rationale: Trough is the lowest concentration, always drawn immediately before the next dose is given.

questions.q: What are the symptoms of an "Upper" overdose?
questions.rationale: Uppers stimulate the CNS; everything goes UP.

questions.q: What is the priority for a "Downer" overdose?
questions.rationale: Downers depress the CNS; too much can stop breathing.

questions.q: What happens during withdrawal from a Downer?
questions.rationale: The body was suppressed; remove the suppressant and the body rebounds upward (seizures, tachycardia).

questions.q: How should Potassium never be administered?
questions.rationale: IV Push potassium causes instant cardiac arrest. Always dilute in a bag.

questions.q: What is the effect of Calcium Channel Blockers?
questions.rationale: They are negative inotropes, chronotropes, and dromotropes (weaken, slow, and block conduction).

questions.q: Why are Beta Blockers contraindicated in Asthma?
questions.rationale: Non-selective beta blockers block beta-2 receptors in the lungs, causing constriction.

questions.q: Which lab monitors Coumadin (Warfarin)?
questions.rationale: PT/INR is for Coumadin. PTT is for Heparin.

questions.q: What patient teaching is needed for Baclofen (Lioresal)?
questions.rationale: It relaxes muscles and sedates the CNS; combining with alcohol enhances sedation.

questions.q: What drug treats Malignant Hyperthermia?
questions.rationale: Dantrolene acts directly on skeletal muscle to stop the rigidity and heat production.

questions.q: How does Kayexalate work?
options: Exchanges Sodium for Potassium in the gut
questions.rationale: It acts as a resin in the bowel, trading sodium ions for potassium ions, which are pooped out.

questions.q: Which insulin can be given IV drip?
questions.rationale: Regular insulin is the only one safe for IV administration.

questions.q: When do you draw a peak level for an IV drug?
questions.rationale: This captures the highest concentration in the blood.

questions.q: When are Standard Precautions used?
questions.rationale: Standard precautions apply to contact with blood, body fluids, non-intact skin, and mucous membranes for all patients.

questions.q: Which diseases require Contact Precautions?
options: MRSA, RSV, Staph, Wound infections, Enteric (C-diff)
questions.rationale: Contact is for anything transmitted by touching skin or surfaces (MRS. WEE mnemonic).

questions.q: Which diseases require Droplet Precautions?
options: Sepsis, Scarlet Fever, Strep, Pertussis, Pneumonia, Flu, Meningitis
questions.rationale: Droplets travel 3 feet; a mask is required. (SPIDERMAN mnemonic).

questions.q: Which diseases require Airborne Precautions?
questions.rationale: Tiny particles float in the air. MTV mnemonic: Measles, TB, Varicella.

questions.q: What is the correct order for putting on PPE?
questions.rationale: Start big (Gown), move up to face (Mask/Goggles), end with hands (Gloves).

questions.q: What is the correct order for removing PPE?
questions.rationale: Remove dirtiest first (Gloves), protect face/airway last (Mask).

questions.q: How often must a restraint order be renewed for an adult?
questions.rationale: Restraints are highly regulated; orders expire daily.

questions.q: What is the COAL mnemonic for canes?
questions.rationale: You hold the cane on the strong side to support the weak side.

questions.q: Use an even-numbered gait (2 or 4) when weakness is...?
questions.rationale: If both legs are weak (bilateral), use 2-point or 4-point. If one leg is broken (unilateral/odd), use 3-point.

questions.q: Who is the priority patient?
questions.rationale: The patient with a new, changing, or unstable condition always comes first.

questions.q: In a tie for priority, use the modifying phrase to find the...?
questions.rationale: If two patients are unstable, prioritize the one with the more vital organ failure (Brain/Lung vs Kidney).

questions.q: Can an LPN perform an admission assessment?
questions.rationale: Admission/Discharge assessments require advanced judgment and planning; RN only.

questions.q: Can a UAP take vitals on a stable patient?
questions.rationale: Routine vitals on stable patients are within the UAP scope.

questions.q: What is the difference between Laminectomy and Laminotomy?
questions.rationale: Ectomy removes the whole lamina; Otomy removes a small part to make space.

questions.q: What is the antidote for Digoxin toxicity?
questions.rationale: Digibind binds to digoxin molecules and inactivates them. Signs of toxicity: nausea, visual changes (green/yellow halos), bradycardia.

questions.q: What is the antidote for Coumadin (Warfarin) overdose?
questions.rationale: Vitamin K reverses Warfarin's anticoagulant effect. Keep PT/INR at 1.5-2 times normal.

questions.q: What is the antidote for Heparin overdose?
questions.rationale: Protamine Sulfate neutralizes Heparin. Keep APTT and PTT at 1.5-2.5 times normal.

questions.q: What is the antidote for opiate/narcotic overdose?
questions.rationale: Narcan (Naloxone) is the opioid antagonist that reverses respiratory depression from morphine, heroin, and other opioids.

questions.q: What is the antidote for Tylenol (Acetaminophen) overdose?
questions.rationale: Mucomyst (N-acetylcysteine) is given as a loading dose plus 17 additional doses to prevent hepatotoxicity from acetaminophen overdose.

questions.q: What is the antidote for Magnesium Sulfate toxicity?
questions.rationale: Calcium Gluconate reverses magnesium toxicity. Signs of Mg toxicity: absent DTRs, respirations <12, urine output <30 mL/hr, fetal bradycardia.

questions.q: What is the antidote for cholinergic medication toxicity (Myasthenic Bradycardia)?
questions.rationale: Atropine is an anticholinergic that blocks excess acetylcholine effects (bradycardia, excessive secretions).

questions.q: What is the antidote for Methotrexate toxicity?
questions.rationale: Leucovorin rescue provides folinic acid that normal cells need, counteracting Methotrexate's folate-blocking effects.

questions.q: What is the therapeutic range for Dilantin (Phenytoin)?
questions.rationale: Dilantin therapeutic range is 10-20 mcg/mL. Side effects include gingival hyperplasia.

questions.q: What is the therapeutic range for Lithium?
questions.rationale: Lithium has a very narrow therapeutic range (0.5-1.5 mEq/L). Blood levels above 1.5 indicate toxicity.

questions.q: What is the therapeutic PT/INR range for a patient on Coumadin?
questions.rationale: Normal PT is 11-12.5 sec. Therapeutic INR on Warfarin is 1.5-2 times normal (INR 2-3 for most conditions).

questions.q: What is the therapeutic APTT/PTT range for a patient on Heparin?
questions.rationale: Normal APTT is 60-70 sec. Therapeutic on Heparin is 1.5-2.5 times the control value.

questions.q: What heart rate must be checked before giving Digoxin?
options: If HR < 60, hold the medication and notify the physician
questions.rationale: Digoxin slows the heart. If apical pulse is below 60 bpm, hold the dose and notify the provider to prevent dangerous bradycardia.

questions.q: What should the nurse teach a patient on Warfarin about diet?
options: Maintain consistent Vitamin K intake; do not change it
questions.rationale: Vitamin K counteracts Warfarin. Don't eliminate it, but keep intake consistent so the dose remains effective and predictable.

questions.q: What drugs should a patient on Warfarin avoid?
options: Aspirin, NSAIDs, and other blood thinners
questions.rationale: Aspirin and NSAIDs inhibit platelet function and increase bleeding risk on top of Warfarin's anticoagulation.

questions.q: What is the normal arterial blood pH range?
questions.rationale: Normal pH is 7.35-7.45. Below 7.35 = acidosis. Above 7.45 = alkalosis.

questions.q: What is the normal PaCO2 range?
questions.rationale: Normal CO2 is 35-45 mmHg. CO2 is the respiratory driver: high CO2 = respiratory acidosis, low CO2 = respiratory alkalosis.

questions.q: What is the normal HCO3 (bicarbonate) range?
questions.rationale: Normal HCO3 is 21-28 mEq/L. HCO3 is the metabolic driver: high = metabolic alkalosis, low = metabolic acidosis.

questions.q: A patient has pH 7.30, CO2 55, HCO3 24. What is this?
questions.rationale: pH is low (acidosis). CO2 is high (the respiratory driver is causing it). HCO3 is normal (not compensating yet). This is uncompensated respiratory acidosis.

questions.q: A patient has pH 7.50, CO2 28, HCO3 24. What is this?
questions.rationale: pH is high (alkalosis). CO2 is low (the patient is hyperventilating, blowing off CO2). HCO3 is normal. This is respiratory alkalosis.

questions.q: What causes a Low Pressure ventilator alarm?
options: Disconnection or leak in the circuit
questions.rationale: Low pressure means air is escaping: disconnected tubing, cuff leak, or loose connections. Check all connections first.

questions.q: What is the first action when any ventilator alarm sounds?
questions.rationale: Always assess the patient first. If the patient is in distress, disconnect the vent and bag manually (Ambu bag) while troubleshooting.

questions.q: What food must be avoided with grapefruit juice interactions?
questions.rationale: Grapefruit inhibits CYP3A4 enzyme, increasing blood levels of many drugs including Ca++ channel blockers and statins to toxic levels.

questions.q: When should statins (Lipitor) be taken?
questions.rationale: Cholesterol synthesis peaks at night, so statins are most effective when taken in the evening. Avoid grapefruit juice.

questions.q: What should a patient do after the first dose of an ACE Inhibitor?
options: Stay in bed for 3 hours due to risk of first-dose hypotension
Take a second dose if BP doesn't drop
questions.rationale: ACE inhibitors can cause severe first-dose hypotension. Stay in bed for 3 hours and rise slowly.

questions.q: What is the common side effect of ACE Inhibitors (-pril drugs)?
questions.rationale: ACE inhibitors (Captopril, Lisinopril, Enalapril) cause a dry, hacking cough due to bradykinin accumulation. Switch to ARB if intolerable.

questions.q: What suffix identifies Beta Blockers?
questions.rationale: Beta blockers end in -olol: Propranolol, Metoprolol, Atenolol. They decrease HR and BP.

questions.q: What suffix identifies ACE Inhibitors?
questions.rationale: ACE inhibitors end in -pril: Captopril, Lisinopril, Enalapril. They block angiotensin-converting enzyme to lower BP.

questions.q: What suffix identifies ARBs (Angiotensin Receptor Blockers)?
questions.rationale: ARBs end in -sartan: Losartan, Valsartan, Irbesartan. They block angiotensin II receptors. Used when ACE inhibitor cough is intolerable.

questions.q: What is the nurse's priority before giving any blood pressure medication?
options: Check blood pressure and heart rate
questions.rationale: Always check BP and HR before giving antihypertensives. Hold if BP or HR are below set parameters and notify the physician.

questions.q: What should the nurse teach about Nitroglycerin (NTG)?
options: Take up to 3 tablets, 5 min apart; if no relief, call 911
questions.rationale: NTG is placed under the tongue (sublingual). Take 1 tablet, wait 5 min, take 2nd, wait 5 min, take 3rd. If still in pain after 3 tabs, call 911.

questions.q: How should Nitroglycerin tablets be stored?
options: In a dark glass container, away from heat and light
questions.rationale: NTG degrades with light, heat, and moisture. Store in original dark glass container with cap tightly closed. Replace every 6 months.

questions.q: What is the normal potassium (K+) range?
questions.rationale: Normal K+ is 3.5-5.0 mEq/L. Both hypo- and hyperkalemia cause dangerous cardiac dysrhythmias.

questions.q: What EKG changes are seen in Hypokalemia?
options: Prominent U waves, depressed ST segment, flat T waves
questions.rationale: Hypokalemia causes flattened T waves, ST depression, and characteristic U waves (extra wave after T wave).

questions.q: What EKG changes are seen in Hyperkalemia?
options: Tall, peaked T waves, prolonged PR interval, widened QRS
Flat T waves and U waves
questions.rationale: Hyperkalemia produces tall, peaked (tented) T waves, widened QRS, and can progress to ventricular fibrillation and cardiac arrest.

questions.q: What is the normal sodium (Na+) range?
questions.rationale: Normal Na+ is 136-145 mEq/L. Sodium goes with water: low Na = fluid excess, high Na = dehydration.

questions.q: What is the normal calcium (Ca++) range?
questions.rationale: Normal Ca++ is 9-10.5 mg/dL. Hypocalcemia causes muscle spasms, tetany, and positive Trousseau's/Chvostek's signs.

questions.q: What are the signs of Hypocalcemia?
options: Muscle spasms, tetany, Trousseau's sign, Chvostek's sign, prolonged QT
questions.rationale: Low calcium causes neuromuscular irritability: spasms, tetany, (+) Trousseau's (carpal spasm with BP cuff), (+) Chvostek's (facial twitch with nerve tap).

questions.q: What does Trousseau's Sign indicate?
options: Hypocalcemia (carpal spasm when BP cuff inflated)
questions.rationale: Trousseau's sign: inflating BP cuff above systolic for 3 minutes causes carpal spasm (hand contraction), indicating hypocalcemia.

questions.q: What does Chvostek's Sign indicate?
options: Hypocalcemia (facial twitch when tapping facial nerve)
questions.rationale: Chvostek's sign: tapping the facial nerve in front of the ear causes ipsilateral facial muscle twitching, indicating hypocalcemia.

questions.q: Which diuretic is potassium-sparing?
questions.rationale: Spironolactone (Aldactone) saves potassium by blocking aldosterone. Watch for hyperkalemia. Also includes Triamterene and Amiloride.

questions.q: Which diuretic wastes potassium and requires potassium monitoring?
questions.rationale: Loop diuretics (Lasix, Bumex) and Thiazides (HCTZ) waste potassium. Monitor K+ levels and supplement as needed.

questions.q: What teaching is important for patients on loop diuretics (Lasix)?
options: Eat potassium-rich foods (bananas, oranges, potatoes) and take in the morning
questions.rationale: Loop diuretics cause K+ loss. Eat potassium-rich foods, take in the morning to avoid nocturia, and monitor for signs of hypokalemia.

questions.q: What is the antidote for benzodiazepine overdose?
questions.rationale: Flumazenil (Romazicon) competitively antagonizes benzodiazepines at the GABA receptor, reversing sedation.

questions.q: What are the 5 Rights of Medication Administration?
options: Right form, color, size, shape, label
Right doctor, nurse, pharmacy, patient, dose
Right drug, patient, room, chart, label
Right patient, drug, dose, route, time
questions.rationale: The 5 Rights: Right Patient, Right Drug, Right Dose, Right Route, Right Time. Always verify all five before giving any medication.

questions.q: What should the nurse do if a medication error occurs?
options: Assess the patient, notify the physician, complete an incident report, and document
Document in the chart and do nothing else
questions.rationale: After a med error: assess the patient first, notify the provider, implement orders, complete an incident/occurrence report, and document the patient's response.

questions.q: What class of drugs ends in -mycin or -micin?
questions.rationale: Aminoglycosides: Gentamicin, Tobramycin, Amikacin. Monitor for ototoxicity (hearing loss/tinnitus) and nephrotoxicity (rising BUN/creatinine).

questions.q: What must be monitored when a patient is on Vancomycin?
options: Trough levels, renal function, and watch for Red Man Syndrome
questions.rationale: Vancomycin is nephrotoxic and ototoxic. Red Man Syndrome (flushing, hypotension) occurs if infused too rapidly. Always infuse over 60+ minutes.

questions.q: What is Red Man Syndrome?
options: Flushing, rash, and hypotension from rapid Vancomycin infusion
questions.rationale: Red Man Syndrome is a histamine-mediated reaction from infusing Vancomycin too quickly. Slow the rate and premedicate with antihistamines.

questions.q: What is the priority assessment before giving a first dose of any antibiotic?
questions.rationale: Always check allergies before antibiotics. Penicillin allergy may cross-react with cephalosporins. Watch for anaphylaxis with the first dose.

questions.q: What is Rifampin used for and what should patients be warned about?
options: Treat TB; turns body fluids rust/orange/red
questions.rationale: Rifampin is a key TB drug that turns urine, tears, sweat, and other body fluids orange/red. It also reduces effectiveness of oral contraceptives.

questions.q: What side effect is associated with Pyridium (Phenazopyridine)?
questions.rationale: Pyridium is a bladder analgesic that turns urine orange/red/pink. It is NOT an antibiotic and only relieves pain. Warn patients about staining.

questions.q: What must be assessed before and after giving an opioid analgesic?
options: Respiratory rate, level of consciousness, and pain level
questions.rationale: Opioids depress respirations. Hold if RR < 12. Assess LOC (sedation precedes resp depression) and pain before/after for effectiveness.

questions.q: At what respiratory rate should the nurse hold an opioid?
questions.rationale: Hold opioids and notify the physician if RR is below 12 breaths/minute. Naloxone (Narcan) should be readily available.

questions.q: What is the common side effect of opioids that requires a standing order?
questions.rationale: Opioids slow GI motility causing constipation. A bowel regimen (stool softeners, laxatives, fluids, fiber) should be started with opioid therapy.

questions.q: What is the Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score that indicates coma?
questions.rationale: GCS < 8 = coma; intubation is often required. GCS ranges from 3 (worst) to 15 (fully alert).

questions.q: What is the RACE protocol for a fire emergency?
options: Rescue patients, Activate alarm, Contain fire (close doors), Extinguish/Evacuate
questions.rationale: RACE: Rescue patients in immediate danger, Activate fire alarm, Contain the fire (close doors/windows), Extinguish if small or Evacuate.

questions.q: What does PASS stand for for fire extinguisher use?
options: Pull pin, Aim at base of fire, Squeeze handle, Sweep side to side
questions.rationale: PASS: Pull the pin, Aim at the base of the fire (not the flames), Squeeze the handle, Sweep side to side.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a trough draw in therapeutic drug monitoring?
options: To measure the lowest drug concentration to ensure it stays above minimum effective level
questions.rationale: The trough level (drawn just before the next dose) confirms the drug level hasn't dropped below the therapeutic minimum.

questions.q: What are the symptoms of Digoxin toxicity?
options: Nausea, vomiting, visual disturbances (green-yellow halos), bradycardia
questions.rationale: Dig toxicity: GI symptoms (nausea, vomiting, anorexia), visual changes (green-yellow halos, blurred vision), and cardiac dysrhythmias (bradycardia). Check K+ (hypokalemia increases toxicity).

questions.q: What electrolyte imbalance increases the risk of Digoxin toxicity?
questions.rationale: Low potassium competes with Digoxin for the same binding sites, increasing cardiac sensitivity to Digoxin.

questions.q: What class of drug should be used cautiously with patients on Warfarin?
options: Antibiotics (many alter Vitamin K production by gut flora, changing INR)
questions.rationale: Many antibiotics kill gut bacteria that produce Vitamin K, potentiating Warfarin's effect and increasing bleeding risk. Monitor INR closely.

questions.q: What patient teaching is essential for Phenytoin (Dilantin)?
options: Good oral hygiene (gingival hyperplasia), do not stop abruptly (seizures), avoid alcohol
Take on an empty stomach only
questions.rationale: Dilantin causes gingival hyperplasia (gum overgrowth) - meticulous oral care needed. Never stop abruptly (status epilepticus). Alcohol lowers seizure threshold.

questions.q: What happens if you abruptly stop an anticonvulsant?
questions.rationale: Abruptly stopping anticonvulsants can trigger rebound seizures including status epilepticus, a medical emergency. Always taper.

questions.q: What is the nurse's role in informed consent?
options: Explain the procedure to the patient
Witness the patient's signature and ensure they understand
Sign on behalf of the patient
questions.rationale: The physician explains the procedure, risks, benefits, and alternatives. The nurse's role is to witness the signature and verify the patient understands.

questions.q: Which task can the nurse NEVER delegate?
questions.rationale: Assessment, teaching, evaluation, and anything requiring clinical judgment are RN-only activities that cannot be delegated to LPNs or UAPs.

questions.q: What tasks can be delegated to an LPN?
options: Admission assessment and care plan development
Routine wound care, medication administration, data collection on stable patients
questions.rationale: LPNs can perform routine skills (wound care, med admin, VS, data collection) on stable patients under RN supervision. They cannot assess, plan, teach, or evaluate.

questions.q: What is the order of priority when using Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs for NCLEX?
options: Physiological needs first, then safety, then love/belonging
questions.rationale: Maslow's: meet physiological needs first (airway, breathing, circulation, food, water), then safety, then psychosocial needs.

questions.q: A patient is prescribed a medication ending in '-pril.' The nurse recognizes this drug class and monitors for:
questions.rationale: -pril = ACE inhibitor (lisinopril, enalapril). Key side effects: persistent dry cough (due to bradykinin accumulation), hyperkalemia, angioedema. Monitor potassium levels and assess for cough.

questions.q: The nurse should hold a medication ending in '-olol' if the patient's:
questions.rationale: -olol = beta-blocker. Beta-blockers lower heart rate. Hold if HR <60 bpm (adult) and notify the provider. Also assess for hypotension, bronchospasm (avoid in asthma), and masked hypoglycemia in diabetics.

questions.q: The therapeutic range for lithium is:
questions.rationale: Lithium therapeutic range is 0.6-1.2 mEq/L. Toxicity begins at >1.5 mEq/L (tremors, confusion, seizures). Draw levels 12 hours after last dose. Ensure adequate sodium and fluid intake — dehydration increases lithium levels.

questions.q: A patient on phenytoin (Dilantin) develops nystagmus and ataxia. The nurse suspects:
options: Normal side effects that will resolve
questions.rationale: Phenytoin therapeutic range is 10-20 mcg/mL. Nystagmus (involuntary eye movement) and ataxia (unsteady gait) are early signs of toxicity. Other signs include gingival hyperplasia and slurred speech. Hold dose and notify provider.

questions.q: A child weighing 33 lbs is ordered 200 mg of a medication. The safe range is 10-20 mg/kg/day. The nurse should:
options: Hold the medication — dose is below the safe range
Hold the medication — dose exceeds the safe range
questions.rationale: 33 lbs / 2.2 = 15 kg. Low: 15 x 10 = 150 mg. High: 15 x 20 = 300 mg. Safe range: 150-300 mg/day. The ordered dose of 200 mg falls within the safe range, so administer as ordered.

questions.q: When a calculated safe dose range reveals the ordered dose is ABOVE the maximum, the nurse should:
options: Hold the medication and notify the provider
Reduce the dose to the maximum safe amount
Administer the dose and monitor closely
Divide the dose into two smaller administrations
questions.rationale: Nurses do NOT independently adjust medication doses. If the ordered dose exceeds the safe range, hold the medication and notify the prescriber. Document the action and the provider's response.

questions.q: When mixing Regular and NPH insulin in the same syringe, the nurse draws up Regular first because:
options: It prevents NPH from contaminating the Regular vial
Regular insulin expires faster once opened
NPH cannot be drawn through a needle
questions.rationale: Drawing clear (Regular) before cloudy (NPH) prevents contamination of the Regular insulin vial with NPH particles. If NPH entered the Regular vial, it would alter the onset time of Regular insulin for all future doses from that vial.

questions.q: Which insulin can NEVER be mixed with other insulins?
questions.rationale: Lantus (glargine) and Levemir (detemir) are long-acting basal insulins that must ALWAYS be given alone — never mixed with other insulins. Their pH formulation would be altered by mixing, changing their duration of action.

questions.q: A patient on an MAOI should avoid ALL of the following EXCEPT:
questions.rationale: Fresh, unprocessed meats are safe. Tyramine builds up in aged, fermented, pickled, and smoked foods. Aged cheese, red wine, sauerkraut, smoked meats, and soy sauce are all high in tyramine and can trigger hypertensive crisis with MAOIs.

questions.q: How long after stopping an MAOI must a patient continue dietary tyramine restrictions?
questions.rationale: MAOIs irreversibly inhibit the MAO enzyme. The body must synthesize new MAO enzyme, which takes 2-4 weeks. Dietary restrictions must continue for this entire period to prevent tyramine crisis even after the drug is discontinued.

questions.q: The nurse is about to administer a medication. Which is the BEST method for patient identification?
options: Ask the roommate to confirm the patient's identity
Check the name on the door
Ask the patient to state their name and date of birth
Verify the room number matches the MAR
questions.rationale: Use at least two patient identifiers (name and DOB) by asking the patient to state them. Never use room number (patients move), door signs, or third-party identification. Compare stated identifiers against the medication administration record and ID band.

questions.q: A patient refuses a prescribed medication. The MOST appropriate nursing action is:
options: Crush the medication and mix it in food
Withhold all other medications as well
Explain that the medication is mandatory
Document the refusal and notify the provider
questions.rationale: Patients have the Right to Refuse medication. Document the refusal, the reason if given, any education provided, and notify the provider. Never force, hide, or coerce medication administration — this violates patient autonomy and is potentially illegal.

questions.q: A patient on warfarin should be taught to:
options: Maintain a consistent daily intake of vitamin K foods
Eat large amounts of broccoli to boost the drug effect
Eliminate all leafy green vegetables from the diet
Take the medication with grapefruit juice
questions.rationale: Vitamin K is the antidote for warfarin. Patients should maintain CONSISTENT vitamin K intake — sudden changes (increasing or decreasing) alter the INR unpredictably. Grapefruit inhibits drug metabolism and should be avoided with many medications.

questions.q: A patient is prescribed a fluoroquinolone antibiotic. The nurse should instruct the patient to avoid taking it with:
questions.rationale: Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin) and tetracyclines bind to calcium, iron, magnesium, and aluminum ions, drastically reducing absorption. Take on an empty stomach, 2 hours before or 6 hours after dairy or antacids.

questions.q: Using the '1s and 10s, 2s and 20s' drug toxicity rule, which TWO drugs become toxic at a level greater than 2?
questions.rationale: Toxic at 2: Lithium (therapeutic 0.6-1.2, toxic >2) and Digoxin (therapeutic 1-2, toxic >2). Toxic at 20: Aminophylline, Dilantin, and Bilirubin.

questions.q: A patient's Aminophylline level is 24 mcg/mL. The nurse should:
options: Increase the dose for better bronchodilation
Hold the medication and notify the HCP
questions.rationale: Aminophylline therapeutic range is 10-20. A level of 24 exceeds the toxic threshold (>20). Hold the drug and notify the provider immediately.

questions.q: A newborn has a bilirubin level of 21 mg/dL. Based on the '2s and 20s' rule, the nurse recognizes this as:
questions.rationale: Bilirubin follows the '2s and 20s' rule: toxic at >20 in newborns. Levels >20 risk kernicterus (brain damage). Phototherapy or exchange transfusion is needed.

questions.q: Which set of therapeutic drug levels is CORRECT?
questions.rationale: Ranges with 1s: Lithium 0.6-1.2, Digoxin 1-2. Ranges with 10s: Aminophylline 10-20, Dilantin 10-20. The '1s and 10s' mnemonic groups all five.

questions.q: A patient on Dilantin (Phenytoin) has a serum level of 8 mcg/mL. The nurse interprets this as:
questions.rationale: Dilantin therapeutic range is 10-20 mcg/mL. A level of 8 is subtherapeutic; the patient is at risk for seizures. Notify HCP for possible dose adjustment.

questions.q: A patient taking Clonidine for hypertension asks why they cannot stop the medication abruptly. The nurse’s best response is:
options: Abrupt discontinuation causes life-threatening rebound hypertension
The drug needs to be tapered to prevent seizures
Abrupt cessation leads to serotonin syndrome
questions.rationale: Clonidine (alpha-2 agonist) must be tapered gradually. Abrupt discontinuation causes dangerous rebound hypertension due to sudden sympathetic surge.

questions.q: Which medication combination is CONTRAINDICATED due to risk of fatal hypotension?
questions.rationale: Sildenafil (Viagra) with Nitroglycerin causes severe, potentially fatal hypotension. This is an absolute contraindication tested frequently on NCLEX.

questions.q: A nurse is preparing to administer Salmeterol (Serevent). Which patient statement indicates a need for further teaching?
options: I will use this inhaler when I have an acute asthma attack
I take this medication every day at the same time
I rinse my mouth after using this inhaler
I use my rescue inhaler for sudden shortness of breath
questions.rationale: Salmeterol is a long-acting beta-agonist (LABA) controller medication — NOT a rescue inhaler. For acute attacks, use Albuterol (SABA). Using Salmeterol for acute symptoms is dangerous.

questions.q: A patient prescribed Sulfamethoxazole/Trimethoprim (Bactrim) reports a history of sulfa allergy. The priority nursing action is:
options: Hold the medication and notify the provider
Give a test dose and monitor for 30 minutes
questions.rationale: Bactrim contains sulfonamide. A sulfa allergy is a contraindication. The nurse must hold the medication and notify the provider for an alternative antibiotic.

questions.q: The nurse is teaching about Doxycycline. Which instruction is MOST important?
options: Avoid prolonged sun exposure and use sunscreen
Take the medication with a full glass of milk
This medication may turn your urine orange
Take on an empty stomach at bedtime
questions.rationale: Doxycycline (tetracycline) causes severe photosensitivity. Patients must avoid sun exposure and use sunscreen. Also: do NOT take with dairy/antacids, and sit upright 30 min after taking.

questions.q: A nurse is teaching a patient about Metronidazole (Flagyl). Which statement by the patient requires immediate follow-up?
options: I will take this with food to reduce nausea
I may notice a metallic taste in my mouth
I plan to have wine with dinner tonight
I will complete the full course of antibiotics
questions.rationale: Metronidazole causes a severe disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol: intense nausea, vomiting, flushing, and tachycardia. Patients must avoid ALL alcohol during treatment and for 48 hours after.

questions.q: Which medication should the nurse hold if the patient’s apical pulse is less than 60 bpm?
questions.rationale: Before administering Digoxin, check the apical pulse for 1 full minute. Hold if HR < 60 bpm (adults) or < 70 bpm (children). Bradycardia is a sign of Digoxin toxicity.

questions.q: A patient on Lamotrigine reports a new skin rash. The priority nursing action is:
options: Document the finding and reassess in 24 hours
Stop the medication and notify the provider immediately
questions.rationale: Lamotrigine carries a Black Box Warning for Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS), a life-threatening skin reaction. Any rash must be reported immediately and the drug discontinued.

questions.q: The antidote for Apixaban or Rivaroxaban (DOAC) toxicity is:
questions.rationale: Andexanet Alfa is the reversal agent for factor Xa inhibitors (Apixaban, Rivaroxaban). Vitamin K reverses Warfarin; Protamine reverses Heparin; Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines.

questions.q: A patient prescribed Omeprazole asks when to take the medication. The nurse responds:
options: Take it 30-60 minutes before your first meal of the day
Take it at bedtime with a snack
Take it with your largest meal
Take it whenever you experience heartburn
questions.rationale: PPIs (Omeprazole, Pantoprazole) must be taken 30-60 minutes before meals for optimal acid suppression. They work by inhibiting the proton pump before it is activated by food.

questions.q: The nurse is monitoring a patient on Clopidogrel (Plavix) scheduled for surgery. Which action is appropriate?
options: Continue the medication through the day of surgery
Discontinue 5-7 days before the scheduled surgery
Switch to Aspirin on the day of surgery
Hold only on the morning of surgery
questions.rationale: Clopidogrel is an antiplatelet that irreversibly inhibits platelet aggregation. It must be discontinued 5-7 days before surgery to reduce the risk of excessive bleeding.

questions.q: Which finding indicates Digoxin toxicity?
questions.rationale: Classic Digoxin toxicity signs: visual disturbances (yellow/green halos), anorexia, nausea/vomiting, bradycardia. Therapeutic range: 0.5-2.0 ng/mL. Antidote: Digoxin Immune Fab (Digibind).

questions.q: A nurse administers Sucralfate to a patient with a peptic ulcer. Which timing is correct?
options: 1 hour before meals on an empty stomach
questions.rationale: Sucralfate acts as a mucosal barrier ('band-aid') that coats the ulcer. It must be taken on an empty stomach, 1 hour before meals, so it can adhere to the ulcer crater without food interference.

questions.q: A patient taking Montelukast (Singulair) reports mood changes and suicidal thoughts. The nurse should:
options: Reassure the patient this is a temporary side effect
Increase the dose for better symptom control
Document and notify the provider immediately
Discontinue the medication and switch to Albuterol
questions.rationale: Montelukast has a Black Box Warning for neuropsychiatric events including mood changes, depression, and suicidal ideation. The provider must be notified immediately for medication evaluation.

questions.q: Which statement about Buspirone is accurate?
options: It causes significant sedation and respiratory depression
It has high potential for dependence and abuse
It can be used as a rescue medication for panic attacks
It takes several weeks to reach full therapeutic effect
questions.rationale: Buspirone is a non-benzodiazepine anxiolytic. Key points: no sedation, no dependence, no respiratory depression — but it takes 2-4 weeks to become effective. Cannot be used PRN for acute anxiety.

questions.q: A patient on Bupropion (Wellbutrin) has a history of bulimia nervosa. The nurse should:
options: Give with food to reduce GI side effects
Question the prescription — this is contraindicated
questions.rationale: Bupropion is contraindicated in patients with eating disorders (bulimia, anorexia) and seizure disorders due to significantly increased seizure risk. The nurse should question this prescription.

questions.q: Which insulin should NEVER be mixed with other insulins?
questions.rationale: Glargine (Lantus) is a long-acting insulin with no peak and 24-hour duration. It must NEVER be mixed with other insulins as this alters its absorption profile. It is given as a separate injection.

questions.q: A patient on Metformin is scheduled for a CT scan with IV contrast dye. The nurse should:
options: Administer Metformin with extra fluids before the procedure
Hold Metformin 48 hours before and after the procedure
Give half the dose on the day of the procedure
questions.rationale: Metformin must be held 48 hours before AND after IV contrast dye procedures to prevent lactic acidosis and acute renal failure. This is a critical NCLEX safety point.

questions.q: Rapid-acting insulin (Lispro) has an onset of 15 minutes. The MOST important nursing intervention is:
options: Ensure the patient eats within 15 minutes of administration
Give it 30 minutes after the meal
Mix it with NPH before injection
questions.rationale: Lispro (rapid-acting) has a 15-minute onset and 1-2 hour peak. The patient MUST eat within 15 minutes to prevent severe hypoglycemia. This is the #1 safety priority.

questions.q: Which lab value is MOST important to monitor for a patient taking Glipizide?
questions.rationale: Glipizide (sulfonylurea) stimulates insulin release from the pancreas. The primary risk is hypoglycemia. Blood glucose must be monitored closely, and patients should know the signs of low blood sugar.

questions.q: A patient on Methimazole for hyperthyroidism reports a sore throat and fever. This may indicate:
options: Expected side effects that will resolve
questions.rationale: Methimazole can cause agranulocytosis (dangerously low WBC count). Sore throat and fever are warning signs. The patient must stop the medication and get a CBC immediately.

questions.q: The nurse is teaching a patient about Atorvastatin (Lipitor). Which instruction is MOST important?
options: Report unexplained muscle pain or weakness immediately
Take the medication in the morning with breakfast
Avoid all dietary fat while on this medication
Expect your cholesterol to normalize within 24 hours
questions.rationale: Statins can cause rhabdomyolysis (skeletal muscle breakdown), a life-threatening condition. Unexplained muscle pain/weakness/dark urine must be reported immediately. Take statins in the evening.

questions.q: Which assessment finding would cause the nurse to hold Metoprolol?
options: Heart rate of 54 bpm and blood pressure of 88/56 mmHg
questions.rationale: Beta-blockers (Metoprolol) are held if HR < 60 bpm or SBP < 100 mmHg. A HR of 54 and BP of 88/56 both meet hold criteria. Also note: beta-blockers mask signs of hypoglycemia in diabetics.

questions.q: Kayexalate (Sodium Polystyrene Sulfonate) is prescribed for a patient. This medication is used to treat:
questions.rationale: Kayexalate binds potassium in the GI tract and removes it through the stool. It is used to treat hyperkalemia. Important: potassium should NEVER be pushed IV — it must always be diluted and infused slowly.

questions.q: A patient on Apixaban asks why they don’t need regular blood tests like their friend on Warfarin. The nurse explains:
options: Apixaban is less effective than Warfarin and doesn’t need monitoring
DOACs like Apixaban have a predictable dose-response and do not require routine lab monitoring
Blood tests for Apixaban are not yet available
Your doctor forgot to order them — you should have monitoring
questions.rationale: Direct oral anticoagulants (Apixaban, Rivaroxaban) have predictable pharmacokinetics and do not require routine INR/aPTT monitoring, unlike Warfarin (INR) or Heparin (aPTT). Monitor for bleeding signs.

questions.q: The nurse is teaching a patient newly prescribed Sertraline (Zoloft). Which information is MOST important?
options: The medication takes 4-6 weeks for full therapeutic effect
You may drive immediately after taking this medication
This medication works best when taken PRN for anxiety
You should increase your tyramine-rich food intake
questions.rationale: SSRIs (Sertraline, Fluoxetine, Citalopram) require 4-6 weeks for full effect. Important: Black Box Warning for suicidal ideation (especially youth). Do not stop abruptly. No tyramine restriction (that's MAOIs).

questions.q: Which medication is the appropriate rescue inhaler for an acute asthma attack?
questions.rationale: Albuterol is a short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) — the first-line rescue inhaler for acute bronchospasm. Fluticasone (ICS) and Salmeterol (LABA) are controllers. Montelukast is for prevention.

questions.q: Which patient on a fluoroquinolone (Ciprofloxacin) should the nurse assess MOST carefully?
questions.rationale: Fluoroquinolones carry a Black Box Warning for tendon rupture, especially in patients >60 years old, those on corticosteroids, and kidney/heart/lung transplant recipients. The elderly walker is at highest risk.

questions.q: Acetylcysteine (Mucomyst) is the antidote for which medication overdose?
questions.rationale: N-acetylcysteine (Mucomyst) is the antidote for Acetaminophen toxicity. It must be given within 8-10 hours of overdose for best results. Acetaminophen max dose: 4g/day. Liver toxicity is the main concern.

questions.q: Which assessment is MOST important before administering an ACE inhibitor (Lisinopril) for the first time?
options: Check blood pressure (risk of first-dose hypotension)
questions.rationale: ACE inhibitors cause first-dose hypotension. Monitor BP closely, especially with the initial dose. Also monitor potassium (risk of hyperkalemia) and watch for dry cough and angioedema.

questions.q: Match the correct antidote: A patient on Warfarin has an INR of 8.5 with signs of bleeding.
questions.rationale: Vitamin K (Phytonadione) reverses Warfarin anticoagulation. Therapeutic INR: 2.0-3.0. An INR of 8.5 indicates severe over-anticoagulation requiring immediate reversal.

questions.q: A patient receiving IV Heparin develops uncontrolled bleeding. The nurse anticipates administering:
questions.rationale: Protamine Sulfate is the specific antidote for Heparin (and partially for Enoxaparin). Vitamin K reverses Warfarin. Calcium Gluconate reverses Magnesium Sulfate toxicity.

questions.q: A patient receiving Magnesium Sulfate for pre-eclampsia shows absent deep tendon reflexes. The nurse should:
options: Continue the infusion and recheck in 1 hour
Stop the infusion and administer Calcium Gluconate
questions.rationale: Absent DTRs signal Magnesium Sulfate toxicity. Stop the infusion immediately and give the antidote: Calcium Gluconate. Also assess respiratory rate (hold if RR < 12) and urine output.

questions.q: The nurse finds an unresponsive patient with pinpoint pupils and a respiratory rate of 6. The priority medication is:
questions.rationale: Pinpoint pupils + respiratory depression = opioid overdose. Naloxone (Narcan) is the opioid antagonist. Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines. These are the two most-tested antidotes on NCLEX.

questions.q: A patient in the ED had an accidental benzodiazepine overdose. The reversal agent is:
questions.rationale: Flumazenil (Romazicon) reverses benzodiazepine sedation and respiratory depression. Use cautiously: can precipitate seizures in chronic benzodiazepine users. Naloxone reverses opioids, not benzos.

questions.q: A child with Digoxin toxicity (level 4.2 ng/mL) requires which antidote?
questions.rationale: Digoxin Immune Fab (Digibind) is the antidote for severe Digoxin toxicity. Therapeutic level: 0.5-2.0 ng/mL. At 4.2, this is critically toxic. Signs: bradycardia, visual halos, nausea, vomiting.

questions.q: The therapeutic range for Lithium is:
questions.rationale: Lithium therapeutic range: 0.6-1.2 mEq/L. Toxicity begins > 1.5 mEq/L. Signs of toxicity: coarse tremors, confusion, diarrhea, vomiting, ataxia. Maintain consistent sodium and fluid intake.

questions.q: A patient on Phenytoin (Dilantin) has a serum level of 25 mcg/mL. The nurse should:
options: Administer the next dose as scheduled
Hold the dose and notify the provider — level is toxic
questions.rationale: Phenytoin therapeutic range: 10-20 mcg/mL. A level of 25 is toxic. Signs: nystagmus, ataxia, slurred speech. Also monitor for gingival hyperplasia. IV Phenytoin must be given with Normal Saline only.

questions.q: The Vancomycin trough level is 22 mcg/mL. The nurse recognizes this is:
options: Above therapeutic range — risk for toxicity
questions.rationale: Vancomycin trough therapeutic range: 10-20 mcg/mL. At 22, this is above range with risk for ototoxicity (hearing loss) and nephrotoxicity (kidney damage). Monitor BUN/Creatinine and hearing.

questions.q: The therapeutic INR range for a patient on Warfarin with atrial fibrillation is:
questions.rationale: Therapeutic INR for standard Warfarin therapy: 2.0-3.0. For mechanical heart valves: up to 3.5. Monitor PT/INR regularly. Antidote: Vitamin K. Maintain consistent Vitamin K dietary intake.

questions.q: Before administering Digoxin, the nurse checks the apical pulse. The pulse is 56 bpm. The nurse should:
options: Administer the medication and recheck in 1 hour
Hold the dose and notify the provider
questions.rationale: Hold Digoxin if apical pulse < 60 bpm (adults). A rate of 56 requires holding the dose and notifying the provider. Always check apical (not radial) pulse for a full 60 seconds before administration.

questions.q: The nurse should hold a beta-blocker (Atenolol) when:
options: Heart rate is 56 bpm or systolic BP is below 100 mmHg
questions.rationale: Hold beta-blockers when HR < 60 bpm or SBP < 100 mmHg. Beta-blockers decrease heart rate and blood pressure. Also important: they mask signs of hypoglycemia in diabetic patients.

questions.q: A patient on Lisinopril develops facial and tongue swelling. The MOST important action is:
options: Elevate the head of bed and monitor
Discontinue the medication and call a rapid response — this is angioedema
Administer the next dose with food
questions.rationale: Angioedema (swelling of face, lips, tongue, airway) is a life-threatening complication of ACE inhibitors. It is a medical emergency requiring immediate discontinuation and emergency intervention.

questions.q: An Oxytocin (Pitocin) infusion is running during labor induction. Contractions are now occurring every 90 seconds. The nurse should:
options: Increase the rate to strengthen contractions
Stop the infusion — contractions are too frequent
questions.rationale: Stop Oxytocin if contractions are < 2 minutes apart or > 90 seconds duration, or if fetal distress occurs. Contractions every 90 seconds (< 2 min apart) indicate uterine hyperstimulation — stop the infusion.

questions.q: A patient presents with shakiness, sweating, confusion, and tachycardia. Blood glucose is 52 mg/dL. The priority intervention is:
options: Give 15 grams of a fast-acting carbohydrate (juice, glucose tablets)
Start an IV normal saline bolus
questions.rationale: These are signs of hypoglycemia (Cold & Clammy = need candy). Give 15g fast-acting carb, recheck BG in 15 minutes. Remember the Rule of 15. If unconscious, give Glucagon IM or Dextrose IV.

questions.q: A patient with polyuria, polydipsia, fruity breath, and blood glucose of 480 mg/dL is MOST likely experiencing:
questions.rationale: The 3 P's (polyuria, polydipsia, polyphagia) + fruity breath + hyperglycemia = DKA. Priority: insulin drip + IV fluids + potassium replacement. Hot & Dry = sugar high. DKA occurs more in Type 1 DM.

questions.q: A patient with weight loss, heat intolerance, exophthalmos, and tachycardia most likely has:
questions.rationale: Hyperthyroidism (Graves'): metabolism is HIGH — weight loss, heat intolerance, tachycardia, exophthalmos. Treatment: PTU, Methimazole, or thyroidectomy. Hypothyroidism is the opposite: weight gain, cold intolerance, lethargy.

questions.q: A nurse is comparing Loop diuretics and Potassium-sparing diuretics. Which statement is correct?
options: Loop diuretics waste potassium; Potassium-sparing diuretics retain potassium
Both types have identical effects on electrolytes
questions.rationale: Loop diuretics (Furosemide) cause hypokalemia (K+ wasting). Potassium-sparing diuretics (Spironolactone) cause hyperkalemia (K+ retention). This distinction is a high-yield NCLEX topic.

questions.q: A patient on Warfarin asks about their diet. The nurse’s BEST response is:
options: Avoid all green leafy vegetables completely
Maintain a CONSISTENT intake of Vitamin K-rich foods
Increase your intake of spinach and broccoli
There are no dietary restrictions with Warfarin
questions.rationale: Patients on Warfarin should maintain CONSISTENT Vitamin K intake — not eliminate it. Sudden changes in Vitamin K consumption alter INR levels. Also: use soft toothbrush, electric razor, report bleeding.

questions.q: A patient taking Alendronate (Fosamax) for osteoporosis should be taught to:
options: Take with dairy products for better calcium absorption
Take at bedtime with a small snack
Take on an empty stomach with water and remain upright for 30 minutes
Crush the tablet for easier swallowing
questions.rationale: Alendronate: take on an empty stomach with a full glass of water, first thing in the morning. Remain upright (sitting or standing) for 30 minutes to prevent esophageal irritation and ulceration.

questions.q: A patient with a MAOI prescription (Phenelzine) needs dietary education. Which food must be AVOIDED?
options: Aged cheese, wine, and cured meats
questions.rationale: MAOIs + Tyramine-rich foods = Hypertensive Crisis. Avoid: aged cheeses, red wine, cured/smoked meats, soy sauce, sauerkraut. This is one of the most dangerous drug-food interactions tested on NCLEX.
